pullRequest.title: Rewrite BulbDevice and rework set_multiple_values(), again
pullRequest.body:
I spent the last few days rewriting BulbDevice, then rewriting it again, and then again, and I now almost have something that I'm almost happy with :rofl:  With all the changes that have been added since #525 I decided to just start the PR over.  In addition to the comments in #525, my current issues are:

1) I feel that `set_white()`, `set_brightness()`, `set_colourtemp()`, `brightness()`, and `colourtemp()` should not exist, at least in their current form.  These functions accept/return different values based on what the bulb type is, forcing the user to know the implementation details for every bulb they use and makes them re-write their code should they ever swap out a bulb.  This goes against the entire point of abstracting away those details.  I would like to get rid of them and move everything over to their respective `*_percentage()` functions instead.

2) Non-underscore versions of `rgb_to_hexvalue()`, `hexvalue_to_rgb()`, and `hexvalue_to_hsv()` have been added, as well as a new `hsv_to_hexvalue()` for completeness.  Instead of a bulb type, these take a boolean `use_rgb` parameter.  This works fine for current bulbs, but my concern is what happens should a new format be introduced?  We can add an additional argument at that time, but I'm wondering if it would be better to replace that boolean with a selector that we can add options to later if needed (options would be, say, `rgb8` and `hsv16` for the current formats).  Thoughts?

3) I hate how  `brightness()`, `colourtemp()`, `colour_rgb()` and `colour_hsv()` all just assume a call to `status()` is going to be both successful and immediately returned (instead of being suck behind other responses that were queued because the user never calls `receive()`).  All the other functions return an error_json object if a call fails to return the expected result, but I don't see how we can do that here as the examples show stuff like `(r, g, b) = colour_rgb()` (which is going to blow up spectacularly and with a nearly-useless error message).  Should these functions be different and throw an exception instead, or should all functions be modified to throw exceptions on unexpected responses?

I don't think there are any breaking changes if people only used function calls, however if they got creative and tried to roll their own queries using/setting DPS_INDEX_* variables (*ahem* #432) then it's gonna break.  ~~I removed all those variables as they're no longer needed, but can add them back if you think it will break less stuff.~~

The bulb capabilities detection should work pretty well with every bulb and dimmer I've seen, but I don't even want to think about what's going to happen if nowait=True is set before the detection finishes.  There are basically 4 ways to set the capabilities/DPs:
1. Call `status()`, either explicitly (with `d.status()`) or implicitly via one of the functions which calls `state()` (which is, like, most of them)
2. Call `detect_bulb()` and either allow it to call `status()`, or provide a status response dps dict as its first parameter.
3. Call `set_bulb_type('x')` and pass it one of the old bulb types (A, B, or C).  It will assume the bulb has all possible capabilities for that bulb type and that they're using the default DP set.
4. Call `set_bulb_capabilities()` with a dict providing a custom DP mapping.  Only the `switch`, `brightness`, `value_min`, and `value_max` keys are required, all the rest are optional.


Speaking of nowait, I've been thinking about adding a non-blocking receive when `nowait=True` where all function calls will return a result if one is waiting, but will not wait for one.  This is to help with people who do stuff like #432 where they can have potentially hundreds of responses waiting in the receive buffer but are never going to call `receive()` to clear them.  Checking for waiting data without blocking can be done using the socket `peek()` function.  My only concern is users who have code which expects to read a response at some point but does not check for one when setting `nowait=True`.  Thoughts?

I currently have 3 functions for music mode: `set_musicmode()`, `unset_musicmode()`, and `set_music_colour()`.  I keep waffling on including set/unset; it basically just modifies (set) and reverts (unset) the `.retry` and `.sendWait` variables to try and make communications as fast as possible.  In my testing the bulb has no problem with a 100ms update time, and sending the command and receiving the ACK averages around 55ms.

Beyond that, there is still some final cleanup needed before merging this, and lots of documentation needs updating too.

Closes #572 
Closes #549 
Closes #432

pullRequest.title: fix: preserve device mappings on transient API failure in getdevices()
pullRequest.body:
## Problem

When `/sync` triggers `getdevices(oldlist=..., include_map=True)` with a freshly-created `Cloud()` instance (empty mapping cache), two mapping-loss scenarios exist:

**1. Changed devices lose old mapping on API failure**
If `getmapping()` encounters a network error or rate limit, it returns `None` without setting `self.mappings[product_id]`. The product_id is absent from the returned `mappings` dict, so the assignment loop never fires. The device exits with no `mapping` key — the old mapping from `oldlist` is silently discarded and then overwritten on the next `tuyaSaveJson()` call.

**2. Unchanged devices have good mappings overwritten by empty results**
The `# also set unchanged devices` loop runs unconditionally. If a changed device returns an empty mapping `{}` (API code 2009 or edge-case empty response), all unchanged devices sharing that `product_id` also lose their mapping.

Fixes #439.

## Fix

Two targeted guards in `getdevices()`:

1. **Guard on unchanged-device overwrite:** only apply the new mapping to unchanged devices if it is non-empty (`if mappings[productid]:`).

2. **Old-mapping fallback for changed devices:** after `getmappings()`, any changed device that still has no mapping gets its old mapping restored from `old_devices` (already built at the top of the function).

No new data structures or API calls are introduced. The `old_devices` dict (built from `oldlist` at the function start) is reused.

pullRequest.title: Add Singapore datacenter, update BulbDevice for non-bulb devices again
Enhance CoverDevice with automatic command type detection
pullRequest.body:
This pull request adds automatic detection of the command type used by `CoverDevice` ("open"/"close" vs "on"/"off"), improving compatibility with different device models and reducing the need for manual configuration. It includes a manual override method, updates documentation and examples, and bumps the project version.

**CoverDevice command detection and configuration:**

* Added lazy detection in `CoverDevice` to automatically determine whether to use "open"/"close" or "on"/"off" commands by checking device status on the first use, defaulting to "on"/"off" for backward compatibility (`tinytuya/CoverDevice.py`).
* Includes `set_cover_command_type(use_open_close=True)` method to allow manual override of the detected command type (`tinytuya/CoverDevice.py`).

**Documentation and examples:**

* Updated function signatures and notes in both the code and `README.md` to document the new command detection behavior and manual override method (`tinytuya/CoverDevice.py`, `README.md`). [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-00c51501260b645f3cb0df02a62315f2af7d79e89b56f14c4aa064e1315321a2L15-R25) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-b335630551682c19a781afebcf4d07bf978fb1f8ac04c6bf87428ed5106870f5L239-R246)
* Added a new example in the `README.md` demonstrating how to use `CoverDevice` with both automatic detection and manual override (`README.md`).

Closes #653

pullRequest.title: Update Scanner - Clarity on v3.4 and v3.5 device identification when unresponsive.
pullRequest.body:
This pull request improves the handling and messaging for devices running Tuya protocol versions 3.4 and 3.5 in the `tinytuya/scanner.py` file. The changes clarify version detection logic, enhance user feedback with more accurate hints about possible device versions, and refine the protocol detection sequence to better distinguish between v3.4 and v3.5 devices.

**Improvements to device version detection and messaging:**

* Updated comments and debug messages to indicate that devices may be running v3.4 or v3.5, not just v3.4, and clarified that version confirmation only occurs upon receiving a valid response.
* Enhanced error and status messages throughout the scanning process to include hints when a device is likely v3.4 or v3.5, improving user feedback when polling fails or unexpected disconnects occur. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-3f2b19dcd5c6d53d5cd1afef116ae4b56c698e8c0b80b329c63a4dd8fd321a63L522-R523) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-3f2b19dcd5c6d53d5cd1afef116ae4b56c698e8c0b80b329c63a4dd8fd321a63L535-R537) [[3]](diffhunk://#diff-3f2b19dcd5c6d53d5cd1afef116ae4b56c698e8c0b80b329c63a4dd8fd321a63L546-R549)

**Refinement of protocol detection logic:**

* Modified the data reading logic to prioritize checking for the v3.5 protocol prefix before falling back to the v3.4 prefix, ensuring more reliable detection of v3.5 devices during scanning. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-3f2b19dcd5c6d53d5cd1afef116ae4b56c698e8c0b80b329c63a4dd8fd321a63L664-R668) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-3f2b19dcd5c6d53d5cd1afef116ae4b56c698e8c0b80b329c63a4dd8fd321a63R678-R683)

Closes #671
